title: Pixel Art Animation
description: Build a tiny scene that comes alive when you press the arrow buttons.
intro:
Build your own pixel-art scene that animates when you press the arrow buttons. You'll fill in a few small functions, then click Run Project to see them all run together.

The canvas is a 20×20 grid. Coordinates are (x, y) from (0, 0) in the bottom-left to (19, 19) in the top-right.

The Setup section below holds variables that any of your functions can read and change — the player's position lives there as player_x and player_y. Add more variables there if you want.
editablePreamble:
player_x = 10
player_y = 10
# you can add more variables here

tasks.title: Colour the corners
tasks.guidance:
Warm-up: draw a coloured cell in each of the four corners of the canvas.

The corners are at (0, 0), (19, 0), (0, 19), and (19, 19). Use draw(x, y, 'colour') — pick whatever colours you like.
tasks.starterBody:
draw(0, 0, 'red')
# add the other three corners

tasks.title: Draw a border
tasks.guidance:
A border goes all the way around the edge of the canvas. There are 76 cells in the border — way too many to type one by one. Use for loops instead.

Tip: you'll need four loops — bottom row, top row, left column, right column. Or one loop with some clever maths!

tasks.title: Draw the background
tasks.guidance:
Time to make the canvas your own. draw_background() is your blank slate — whatever you draw here sits behind the player.

Start by calling the two functions you've already written (draw_corners() and draw_border()), then add anything else you can think of. A few ideas to get you started:

  • A row of coloured rectangles along the bottom for grass or sand
  • Rainbow stripes across the middle
  • A pattern of dots
  • A shape using nested loops — a triangle, diamond, or letter
  • Anything else you can imagine

There's no automatic check on this one — it's your space to be creative.
tasks.starterBody:
draw_corners()
draw_border()
# add your own stuff! rectangles, stripes, shapes, anything.

tasks.title: Draw the player
tasks.guidance:
The player lives at player_x, player_y — the Setup section starts these at (10, 10).

Draw something to represent the player at that position. The simplest version is a single draw(player_x, player_y, 'yellow'). Want a bigger character? Draw multiple cells around the centre.

tasks.title: Build the scene
tasks.guidance:
This function runs every time you press a button. Its job is to put the whole picture together by calling your other functions.

Order matters — later draws appear on top of earlier ones. Background first, player last.

tasks.title: Left button: do something cool
tasks.guidance:
Runs when the player clicks the ← button. The simplest thing: move the player one cell to the left.

player_x -= 1 subtracts one from player_x. draw_scene() will run again automatically afterwards, so the player visibly moves.

Why the global line? Without it, Python thinks player_x is a brand-new variable inside this function. global player_x tells Python “the player_x I mean is the one from Setup — change that one”. You'll need a global line at the top of any function that changes a Setup variable (just reading is fine without it).

Want to do something else cool instead? Paint a streak across the canvas, change a colour somewhere — up to you.

tasks.title: Right button: do something cool
tasks.guidance: Runs when the player clicks the → button. Mirror of left — or do something completely different.

tasks.title: Up button: do something cool
tasks.guidance: Runs when the player clicks the ↑ button. Remember: in our coordinate system, up means a bigger player_y.

tasks.title: Down button: do something cool
tasks.guidance: Runs when the player clicks the ↓ button. Down means a smaller player_y.
